```
julia> s = "CompletionFoo.test(1, 1, "
"CompletionFoo.test(1, 1, "

julia> Base.incomplete_tag(Meta.parse(s, raise=false, depwarn=false))
:none
```
vs Base:
```
julia> Base.incomplete_tag(Meta.parse(s, raise=false, depwarn=false))
:other
```

I looked into adjusting the tag selector a bit, but couldn't come up with a good version that also distinguished `[x ` (with space), `[x)` and `(` and `( `, so there may need to be some adjustment to the source-location representation itself. @c42f can you take a {"repository_id":430564540,"push_id":21573712287,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/caf/fix-incomplete-tag-with-whitespace","head":"9fcc90394a459713930cbf253237def03472a98b","before":"e5e19c148908c83fc4ca9810b85dc04380321544","commits":[{"sha":"9fcc90394a459713930cbf253237def03472a98b","author":{"email":"0d1bb7fa6b1aefd25edab787ad0bbdf687df277d@gmail.com","name":"Claire Foster"},"message":"Fix incomplete_tag generation with trailing whitespace

Previously we relied on the last SyntaxNode consuming all trailing
whitespace when detecting incomplete syntax. However this assumption was
broken by #397 and is generally fragile with respect to any extra

```
julia> s = "CompletionFoo.test(1, 1, "
"CompletionFoo.test(1, 1, "

julia> Base.incomplete_tag(Meta.parse(s, raise=false, depwarn=false))
:none
```
vs Base:
```
julia> Base.incomplete_tag(Meta.parse(s, raise=false, depwarn=false))
:other
```

I looked into adjusting the tag selector a bit, but couldn't come up with a good version that also distinguished `[x ` (with space), `[x)` and `(` and `( `, so there may need to be some adjustment to the source-location representation itself. @c42f can you take a look?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/512/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/512/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/comments/2522412222","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/512#issuecomment-2522412222","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/512","id":2522412222,"node_id":"IC_kwDOGankvM6WWPS-","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-06T07:55:05Z","updated_at":"2024-12-06T07:55:05Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"@Keno I think this is fixed by #518. {"repository_id":430564540,"push_id":21598821571,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"2d19c068cc942005db589ac25edc6b32fe5be8f8","before":"9483de85a3e59f017330f6f36a083e7951262e87","commits":[{"sha":"2d19c068cc942005db589ac25edc6b32fe5be8f8","author":{"email":"0d1bb7fa6b1aefd25edab787ad0bbdf687df277d@gmail.com","name":"Claire Foster"},"message":"Fix incomplete_tag generation with trailing whitespace (#518)

Previously we relied on the last SyntaxNode consuming all trailing
whitespace when detecting incomplete syntax. However this assumption was
broken by #397 and is generally fragile with respect to any extra
bump_trivia() calls. Fix this by just comparing to the stream position
before any bumping of remaining trivia.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/commits/2d19c068cc942005db589ac25edc6b32fe5be8f8"}]}

```
julia> s = "CompletionFoo.test(1, 1, "
"CompletionFoo.test(1, 1, "

julia> Base.incomplete_tag(Meta.parse(s, raise=false, depwarn=false))
:none
```
vs Base:
```
julia> Base.incomplete_tag(Meta.parse(s, raise=false, depwarn=false))
:other
```

I looked into adjusting the tag selector a bit, but couldn't come up with a good version that also distinguished `[x ` (with space), `[x)` and `(` and `( `, so there may need to be some adjustment to the source-location representation itself. @c42f can you take a look?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/512/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/512/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

{"action":"opened","number":522,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pulls/522","id":2248206456,"node_id":"PR_kwDOGankvM6GAOh4","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/522","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/522.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/522.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/522","number":522,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Make left hand side of `->` parse as a tuple of arguments","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This makes argument lists on the left hand side of `->` parse as tuples. In particular, this fixes a very inconsistent case where the left hand side previously parsed as a `K"block"`; we now have:

* `(a;x=1)->b` ==> `(-> (tuple-p a (parameters (= x 1))) b`

rather than `(block a (= x 1))` on the left hand side.

In addition, the following forms are treated consistently

* `a->b`       ==> `(-> (tuple a) b)`
* `(a)->b`     ==> `(-> (tuple-p a) b)`
* `(a,)->b`    ==> `(-> (tuple-p-, a) b)`

The upside of this is that expression processing of `->` syntax should be much easier.

There's one aberrant case involving `where` which is particularly difficult and still not dealt with:

`(x where T) -> b` does not parse as `(where (tuple x) T)` on the left hand side. However, `where` precedence involving `::` and `->` is already horribly broken and this syntax will always be awful to write unless we make breaking changes. So I'm too tempted to call this a lost cause for now 😬.

Compatibility shims for converting the `SyntaxNode` form back to `Expr` in order to keep `Expr` stable are included. (At some point we should consider fixing this and deleting these shims because the new form is so much more consistent and would be reflected neatly into `Expr` {"repository_id":430564540,"push_id":21878844956,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"4a7e8462b31e289c05bae43a5f029b0c2e2fccbb","before":"5ff59c28b021490cc3c8b3bac3fc1841209844bd","commits":[{"sha":"4a7e8462b31e289c05bae43a5f029b0c2e2fccbb","author":{"email":"0d1bb7fa6b1aefd25edab787ad0bbdf687df277d@gmail.com","name":"Claire Foster"},"message":"Make left hand side of `->` a tuple of arguments (#522)

This makes argument lists on the left hand side of `->` parse as tuples.
In particular, this fixes a very inconsistent case where the left hand
side previously parsed as a `K"block"`; we now have:

* `(a;x=1)->b` ==> `(-> (tuple-p a (parameters (= x 1))) b`

rather than `(block a (= x 1))` on the left hand side.

In addition, the following forms are treated consistently

* `a->b`       ==> `(-> (tuple a) b)`
* `(a)->b`     ==> `(-> (tuple-p a) b)`
* `(a,)->b`    ==> `(-> (tuple-p-, a) b)`

The upside of this is that expression processing of `->` syntax should
be much easier.

There's one aberrant case involving `where` which is particularly
difficult and still not dealt with:

`(x where T) -> b` does not parse as `(where (tuple x) T)` on the left
hand side. However, `where` precedence involving `::` and `->` is
already horribly broken and this syntax will always be awful to write
unless we make breaking changes. So I'm too tempted to call this a lost
cause for now 😬.

Compatibility shims for converting the `SyntaxNode` form back to `Expr`
in order to keep `Expr` stable are included. (At some point we should
consider fixing this and deleting these shims because the new form is so
much more consistent and would be reflected neatly into `Expr` form.)","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/commits/4a7e8462b31e289c05bae43a5f029b0c2e2fccbb"}]}

{"repository_id":430564540,"push_id":21908698225,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/caf/operator-tokens-as-identifiers","head":"33f6dfb433695df587d201129889f7df9ce63f51","before":"3155f513b8db2dbd0c6b34ad5e606b83b212d4d6","commits":[{"sha":"33f6dfb433695df587d201129889f7df9ce63f51","author":{"email":"0d1bb7fa6b1aefd25edab787ad0bbdf687df277d@gmail.com","name":"Claire Foster"},"message":"Parse non-syntactic operator tokens as `K"Identifier"` kind

Most operators are semantically just normal identifiers after parsing so
get the Kind `K"Identifier"`. For example, `a + b` parses with
`K"Identifier"` kind for the `+` token.

As an exception, standalone syntactic ops keep their kind - they can't
really be used in a sane way as identifiers or interpolated into
expressions in the normal way because they have their own syntactic
forms. This also helps us in `Expr` conversion where they also have
their own rules for coalescing with dots, when dotted.

Also introduce a new keyword `operators_as_identifiers` to the
`tokenize()` API to accommodate some simple uses of this API to colour
token strings by operator type, even when the operator is semantically
in identifier-position.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/commits/33f6dfb433695df587d201129889f7df9ce63f51"}]}

For example, `a + b` parses with `K"Identifier"` kind for the `+` token.

As an exception, standalone syntactic ops keep their kind - they can't really be used in a sane way as identifiers or interpolated into expressions in the normal way because they have their own syntactic forms. This also helps us in `Expr` conversion where they also have their own rules for coalescing with dots, when dotted.

Also introduce a new keyword `operators_as_identifiers` to the `tokenize()` API to accommodate some simple uses of this API to colour token strings by operator type, even when the operator is semantically in identifier-position.

Fix #474 as a follow-on from #456

CC @fredrikekre {"action":"closed","number":523,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pulls/523","id":2255065047,"node_id":"PR_kwDOGankvM6GaY_X","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/523","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/523.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/523.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/523","number":523,"state":"closed","locked":false,"title":"Parse non-syntactic operator tokens as `K"Identifier"` kind","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"Most operators are semantically just normal identifiers after parsing so should get the Kind `K"Identifier"`. For example, after this change `a + b` parses with `K"Identifier"` kind for the `+` token.

As an exception, standalone syntactic ops keep their kind - they can't really be used in a sane way as identifiers or interpolated into expressions in the normal way because they have their own syntactic forms. This also helps us in `Expr` conversion where they also have their own rules for coalescing with dots, when dotted.

Also introduce a new keyword `operators_as_identifiers` to the `tokenize()` API to accommodate some simple uses of this API to colour token strings by operator type, even when the operator is semantically in identifier-position.

Fix #474 as a follow-on from #456

CC @fredrikekre various operator token kinds is relevant during parsing, but they should potentially all be remapped to `K"Identifier"` afterward because their special status as operators is no longer meaningful once they live in the parse tree - they are essentially just identifiers which will be looked up with the usual scoping rules, just like any other identifier.

Code which processes syntax trees often has to do things like `is_operator(x) || kind(x) == K"Identifier"`. Mapping them to identifiers simplifies things and fixes this issue. For the same reasons, I've also wished for this in JuliaLowering.jl.

One caveat is that this change might be unhelpful for the `JuliaSyntax.tokenize()` API - need to think about what to do there.  (Perhaps operator kinds should be preserved in that case?)

If we do this, we should also do it systematically. For example, to fix https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/456/files#r1692517031","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/474/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/474/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

{"repository_id":430564540,"push_id":21916623550,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"35dc3bcebf9a1fe291cdc3865cb0cf018df806a6","before":"4a7e8462b31e289c05bae43a5f029b0c2e2fccbb","commits":[{"sha":"35dc3bcebf9a1fe291cdc3865cb0cf018df806a6","author":{"email":"0d1bb7fa6b1aefd25edab787ad0bbdf687df277d@gmail.com","name":"Claire Foster"},"message":"Parse non-syntactic operator tokens as `K"Identifier"` kind (#523)

Most operators are semantically just normal identifiers after parsing so
should get the Kind `K"Identifier"`. For example, after this change `a + b`
parses with `K"Identifier"` kind for the `+` token.

As an exception, standalone syntactic ops keep their kind - they can't
really be used in a sane way as identifiers or interpolated into
expressions in the normal way because they have their own syntactic
forms. This also helps us in `Expr` conversion where they also have
their own rules for coalescing with dots, when dotted.

Also introduce a new keyword `operators_as_identifiers` to the
`tokenize()` API to accommodate some simple uses of this API to colour
token strings by operator type, even when the operator is semantically
in identifier-position.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/commits/35dc3bcebf9a1fe291cdc3865cb0cf018df806a6"}]}

stage)"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-11-20T04:32:36Z","updated_at":"2024-11-20T04:32:36Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"In Julia 1.11, using `const` in front of a variable implied neither `local` nor `global` semantics - rather the scope was calculated according to the usual rules:

```julia
julia> Meta.@lower function f()
           const x = 1
       end
:($(Expr(:error, "unsupported `const` declaration on local variable around REPL[1]:2")))

julia> let
           const x = 1
       end
ERROR: syntax: unsupported `const` declaration on local variable around REPL[2]:2
```

However, in 1.12 dev, as of https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/pull/54773, it seems that `const` implicitly implies `global`:

```julia
julia> Meta.@lower function f()
           const x = 1
       end
:($(Expr(:error, "`global const` declaration not allowed inside function around REPL[3]:2")))

julia> let
           const x = 1
       end
1

julia> x
1
```

This seems like a significant semantic change which I'm not convinced is a good idea. If one wants a `global` variable within a `let` block, I think the user should explicitly declare `global const x = 1` in the example above.

@Keno is this a bug or was it intentional?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56613/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56613/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

This seems like a significant semantic change which I'm not convinced is a good idea. If one wants a `global` variable within a `let` block at top level, I think the user should explicitly declare `global const x = 1` in the example above.

@Keno is this a bug or was it intentional?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56613/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56613/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2487473734","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56613#issuecomment-2487473734","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56613","id":2487473734,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6UQ9ZG","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-20T05:18:08Z","updated_at":"2024-11-20T05:18:08Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> It is intentional that the const intermediate form is available from local scope. I'm fine retaining the error for the const syntax form, though in that case we should probably rename the syntax head for the former.

Sure. I would like to change the IR form for `const` declarations.

Currently it is `Expr(:const, GlobalRef(mod, varname), value)` which feels uncomfortably close to the surface syntactic form `Expr(:const)`.

In JuliaLowering I'd prefer to use a dedicated head for this. We already have `Expr(:globaldecl)` so the obvious possibility is to have a `Expr(:constdecl)`:

```julia
Expr(:constdecl, GlobalRef(mod, varname), value)
```

Alternatively, fold together `Expr(:globaldecl)` and `Expr(:constdecl)` somehow to have a single IR form for atomically declaring/initializing globals.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2487473734/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I have a function with a nested type delegation (two `where`s), I get the following incorrect warning

```Julia
julia> foo(;a::T) where {T<:AbstractVector{N}} where {N} = zero(N)
WARNING: method definition for foo at REPL[5]:1 declares type variable N but does not use it.
WARNING: method definition for foo##kw at REPL[5]:1 declares type variable N but does not use it.
foo (generic function with 1 method)
```

It is being used, as can be seen by these two executions of the function
```Julia
julia> foo(a=[1.0])
0.0

julia> foo(a=[1])
0
```

If I do something similar without nesting the type, it works fine with no warning
```Julia
julia> bar(;a::N) where {N} = zero(N)
bar (generic function with 1 methods)
```

I just noticed this bug while porting the code to JuliaLowering.jl and set out to prove it ... and landed here :)

This is fixed in the JuliaLowering.jl implementation - the fix, conceptually, is to traverse the graph of symbolic dependencies between type variables, only picking up type variables which are used directly in the function arguments, or transitive dependencies of such.

The broken flisp lowering code for default args case is here:

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/858cb629e9ed476a4f76662676366400b95e5ced/src/julia-syntax.scm#L655","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2492793362/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56002","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56002/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56002/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56002/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56002","id":2567866417,"node_id":"I_kwDOABkWpM6ZDogx","number":56002,"title":"Shadowing in closures in `let` blocks influences bindings in neighboring functions","user":{"login":"Seelengrab","id":11753998,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExNzUzOTk4","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11753998?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab","html_url":"https://github.com/Seelengrab","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Seelengrab/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":1363386,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwxMzYzMzg2","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/breaking","name":"breaking","color":"802000","default":false,"description":"This change will break code"},{"id":371587497,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWwzNzE1ODc0OTc=","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/labels/design","name":"design","color":"e99695","default":false,"description":"Design of APIs or of the language itself"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":16,"created_at":"2024-10-05T11:02:34Z","updated_at":"2024-11-30T20:59:14Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"MWE:

```julia
julia> let; 
           function a()
               b()
           end
           function b()
               b = 1
               return b
           end
           @show a()
           @show a()
       end
a() = 1
ERROR: MethodError: objects of type Int64 are not callable
The object of type `Int64` exists, but no method is defined for this combination of argument types when trying to treat it as a callable object.
Maybe you forgot to use an operator such as *, ^, %, / etc. ?
Stacktrace:
 [1] (::var"#a#a##6")()
   @ Main ./REPL[9]:3
 [2] top-level scope
   @ REPL[9]:10
 [3] macro expansion
   @ show.jl:1229 [inlined]
```

As far as I can tell, what's going on is that `a()` and `b()` end up sharing the closed-over `b` in `b()`; either that, or the assignment in `b()` ends up overwriting the binding to `b` in the `let` block, after which the call to `b()` in `a()` fails due to the new object not being a function.

I'd expect this to behave the same as without the `let` block, that is, just print `a() = 1` twice. This was found by a user of Supposition.jl, downstream issue is https://github.com/Seelengrab/Supposition.jl/issues/54. To be clear, the shadowing in `b()` is fine, it's just surprising/unexpected/undesirable to have that shadowing influence what happens in `a()` (which shouldn't care about the shadowing in `b`, and just call the function).

{"action":"opened","number":511,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pulls/511","id":2109190761,"node_id":"PR_kwDOGankvM59t7Jp","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/511","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/511.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/511.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/511","number":511,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Parse docstrings within structs as `K"doc"`","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Julia's ecosystem (including Base.Docs and flisp lowering) assumes that strings within `struct` definitions are per-field docstrings, but the flisp parser doesn't handle these - they are only recognized when the struct itself has a docstring and are processed by the `@doc` macro recursing into the struct's internals. For example, the following doesn't result in any docs attached to `A`.

```julia
struct A
    "x_docs"
    x

    "y_docs"
    y
end
```

This change adds `K"doc"` node parsing to the insides of a struct, making the semantics clearer in the parser tree and making it possible to address this problems in the future within JuliaLowering.

{"repository_id":430564540,"push_id":20571626104,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"98c202a549aba583aeb7796af37d4cdf0b11eb86","before":"32b80e3ed6f89e399b8af2e668bf926fe58f464e","commits":[{"sha":"98c202a549aba583aeb7796af37d4cdf0b11eb86","author":{"email":"0d1bb7fa6b1aefd25edab787ad0bbdf687df277d@gmail.com","name":"Claire Foster"},"message":"Parse docstrings within structs as `K"doc"` (#511)

Julia's ecosystem (including Base.Docs and flisp lowering) assumes that
strings within `struct` definitions are per-field docstrings, but the
flisp parser doesn't handle these - they are only recognized when the
struct itself has a docstring and are processed by the `@doc` macro
recursing into the struct's internals. For example, the following
doesn't result in any docs attached to `A`.

```julia
struct A
    "x_docs"
    x

    "y_docs"
    y
end
```

This change adds `K"doc"` node parsing to the insides of a struct,
making the semantics clearer in the parser tree and making it possible
to address this problems in the future within JuliaLowering.

Also ensure that the `Expr` form is unaffected by this change.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/commits/98c202a549aba583aeb7796af37d4cdf0b11eb86"}]}

`K"doc"`","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-10-05T22:44:18Z","updated_at":"2024-10-06T00:50:58Z","closed_at":"2024-10-06T00:49:35Z","author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pulls/511","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/511","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/511.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/511.patch","merged_at":"2024-10-06T00:49:35Z"},"body":"Julia's ecosystem (including Base.Docs and flisp lowering) assumes that strings within `struct` definitions are per-field docstrings, but the flisp parser doesn't handle these - they are only recognized when the struct itself has a docstring and are processed by the `@doc` macro recursing into the struct's internals. For example, the following doesn't result in any docs attached to `A`.

```julia
struct A
    "x_docs"
    x

    "y_docs"
    y
end
```

This change adds `K"doc"` node parsing to the insides of a struct, making the semantics clearer in the parser tree and making it possible to address this problems in the future within JuliaLowering.

Also ensure that the `Expr` form is unaffected by this change.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/511/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/511/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/comments/2395244180","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/511#issuecomment-2395244180","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/511","id":2395244180,"node_id":"IC_kwDOGankvM6OxIaU","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-06T00:50:57Z","updated_at":"2024-10-06T00:50:57Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"I think this should be pretty safe and reasonable (comments welcome though)

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/509","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/509/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/509/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/509/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/509","id":2551768490,"node_id":"I_kwDOGankvM6YGOWq","number":509,"title":"Macros juxtaposed with strings incorrectly(?) get raw string literal","user":{"login":"NHDaly","id":1582097,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE1ODIwOTc=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1582097?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly","html_url":"https://github.com/NHDaly","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/NHDaly/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":3571867784,"node_id":"LA_kwDOGankvM7U5mCI","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/labels/bug","name":"bug","color":"d73a4a","default":true,"description":"Something isn't working"}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-09-27T00:35:49Z","updated_at":"2024-10-07T11:31:27Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"If you directly juxtapose a macro with a string, the macro is supplied a raw literal version of the string **as if the macro were a string macro,** even though it's not.

Here is an example:
```julia
julia> macro m(x)
           return esc(x)
       end
@m (macro with 1 method)

julia> @m "hey $(2+2)"
"hey 4"

julia> @m"hey $(2+2)"
"hey \$(2+2)"

julia> m"hey $(2+2)"
ERROR: LoadError: UndefVarError: `@m_str` not defined
in expression starting at REPL[4]:1
```
I  **think** this is a bug. On julia 1.6, which I happen to have installed and which doesn't yet have JuliaSyntax, we get this error instead:
```julia
julia> VERSION
v"1.6.7"

julia> @m"hey $(2+2)"
ERROR: syntax: invalid macro usage "@(@m_str # REPL[4], line 1 "hey $(2+2)")"
Stacktrace:
 [1] top-level scope
   @ none:1
```

-------

I believe the mistake is that the macro is being parsed as if it's a string macro, since the input matches what a string macro would see:
```julia
julia> macro m_str(x)
           return esc(x)
       end
@m_str (macro with 1 method)

julia> m"hey $(2+2)"
"hey \$(2+2)"
```

---------


I see the current behavior on both julia 1.10 and 1.12:
```julia
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.12.0-DEV.1173
Commit 169e9e8de1* (2024-09-09 15:10 UTC)
Platform Info:
  OS: macOS (arm64-apple-darwin23.5.0)
  CPU: 12 × Apple M2 Max
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LLVM: libLLVM-18.1.7 (ORCJIT, apple-m2)
Threads: 1 default, 0 interactive, 1 GC (on 8 virtual cores)
Environment:
  JULIA_SSL_CA_ROOTS_PATH = 
```
```julia
julia> versioninfo()
Julia Version 1.10.2
Commit bd47eca2c8a (2024-03-01 10:14 UTC)
Build Info:
  Official https://julialang.org/ release
Platform Info:
  OS: macOS (arm64-apple-darwin22.4.0)
  CPU: 12 × Apple M2 Max
  WORD_SIZE: 64
  LIBM: libopenlibm
  LLVM: libLLVM-15.0.7 (ORCJIT, apple-m1)
Threads: 1 default, 0 interactive, 1 GC (on 8 virtual cores)
Environment:
  JULIA_SSL_CA_ROOTS_PATH = 
```

In this case, it fails so we need to fix that.

```julia
julia> collect(JuliaSyntax.Tokenize.tokenize("@m\"str\$x\""))
6-element Vector{JuliaSyntax.Tokenize.RawToken}:
 0-0        @              
 1-1        Identifier     
 2-2        "              
 3-7        String         
 8-8        "              
 9-8        EndMarker      

julia> collect(JuliaSyntax.Tokenize.tokenize("@m \"str\$x\""))
9-element Vector{JuliaSyntax.Tokenize.RawToken}:
 0-0        @              
 1-1        Identifier     
 2-2        Whitespace     
 3-3        "              
 4-6        String         
 7-7        $              
 8-8        Identifier     
 9-9        "              
 10-9       EndMarker
```

I think it'd be good enough to track the two previous tokens and check whether one was an `@` - this is probably good enough in practice.

(Unfortunately, Julia also allows syntax like `@A.B.C.x"str"` to mean `A.B.C.@x"str"` and making that also work would need feedback from the parser state to the lexer (uuugh!) but I really feel the `@` in front of the module name is horrible syntax and should be deprecated!)","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/comments/2396668625/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

{"repository_id":430564540,"push_id":19592364188,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"d2e61f01485e4483baa241b34da5b83b7fb1f924","before":"ebda0828f631e3d8dc6ceb4ee5c0c44139563cd4","commits":[{"sha":"d2e61f01485e4483baa241b34da5b83b7fb1f924","author":{"email":"0d1bb7fa6b1aefd25edab787ad0bbdf687df277d@gmail.com","name":"Claire Foster"},"message":"Fix premature lowering of cmd strings (#480)

Unadorned backtick command syntax was prematurely lowered to a macrocall
in the parser with the `core_@cmd` macro name. Remove this special macro
name (almost the last of the special zero-width tokens to be removed!)
and rely instead on the presence of unadorned `cmdstring` to do the
lowering to a `Core.@cmd` call later during Expr conversion.

Also some clean up `Kind`s, grouping them more sensibly and removing the
obsolete kinds `K"core_@int128_str"` `K"core_@uint128_str"` `K"core_@big_str"`.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/commits/d2e61f01485e4483baa241b34da5b83b7fb1f924"}]}

are four macrocalls to special `Kind`s which are arguably premature lowering.

```
K"core_@cmd"
K"core_@int128_str"
K"core_@uint128_str"
K"core_@big_str"
```

In the same way that we removed `K"core_@doc` previously (see #217), these should have their own kinds instead: 

```
K"cmdstring" # ??
K"Int128"
K"UInt128"
K"BigInt"
```

For `K"core_@cmd"` there might be some thought required: it could be either
* A single `K"CmdString"`, with the delimiters as separate trivia tokens in the parent?
* A `K"cmdstring"` container - in analogy to `K"string"` - with a `K"String"` or `K"CmdString"` chunk inside?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/438/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/438/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/481","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/481/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/481/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/481/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/481","id":2446702998,"node_id":"I_kwDOGankvM6R1bmW","number":481,"title":"Managing releases and version numbers here vs Base","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":0,"created_at":"2024-08-04T01:30:11Z","updated_at":"2024-08-04T01:30:11Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"We need a clear procedure for managing JuliaSyntax releases.

This is currently awkward because we're basically versioning two APIs:

1. There's the JuliaSyntax package version for our `SyntaNode` AST and APIs like `parsestmt()`, etc. Next release will be #472, we have a couple more breaking changes to make.
2. There's the version number for `Expr`-related functionality used by Base - this is semantically just Julia version number (apart from bugfixes specific to this package)

These two versions serve really different purposes and may not even have compatible orderings.

For example, #456 made a change to operator kinds in the AST which is completely invisible to `Expr`, but is a breaking change to the JuliaSyntax API and should be followed on with #474 for consistency before the next JuliaSyntax release.  Conversely, the new functionality introduced by #456 is only a feature addition.

There's one big mitigating factor: JuliaSyntax's `parsestmt()` has a `version` which is independent of Julia's `VERSION`, so in principle any new version of JuliaSyntax can be used with an old version of Julia.

I hope that getting to a solid JuliaSyntax 1.0.0 release soon will get things less diverged and easier to manage.

There's one extra factor - for the purposes of maintaining a Julia LTS, we might want an LTS branch of JuliaSyntax itself to support the associated Julia LTS. This is partly a sign that our testing still needs work but being conservative with patches for the LTS is probably a good thing.

### What to do about the mess?

How about we do the following:
* Get JuliaSyntax to 1.0 asap, and try to cut releases off `main` more often. If we need a 2.0 soon, so be it (we will; JuliaSyntax's API will keep evolving, it's not close to done yet.
* Potentially, keep an release-lts-1.10 branch for Julia 1.10 LTS
* Use the latest JuliaSyntax releases when vendoring into Julia Base dev versions

This last one is a bit of an unknown. We don't want to hold Julia Base development up by having JuliaSyntax's release cycle stuck like it has been lately. So alternatively we could just tag JuliaSyntax with a Base-specific version tag at any time that someone bumps the vendored version in Base.  But then we have even more versions flying around and that seems bad too.

### Tooling

* There's a hacky script I use for bumping the vendored version in Base. This should at least go in this repo in the tools directory. Or maybe in Base itself.
* It'd be nice to have regression tests of JuliaSyntax against itself for both `SyntaxNode` and for `Expr`. @LilithHafner had some good ideas about this. At some point we also need to stop testing against the flisp parser as the source of truth. Especially as we add features, but also because fixing bugs in things like line numbers makes maintaining the code which compares with the flisp parser pretty tedious and error prone.
* We really need better tooling for regression testing against the corpus of Julia code in the package ecosystem. We should be doing this for `Expr` parsing (at least) each time we release a JuliaSyntax version. At the moment this is a problem because downloading and unpacking the entire package ecosystem is slow. {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/481","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/481/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/481/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/481/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/481","id":2446702998,"node_id":"I_kwDOGankvM6R1bmW","number":481,"title":"Managing releases and version numbers here vs Base","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-08-04T01:30:11Z","updated_at":"2024-08-04T02:48:37Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"We need a clear procedure for managing JuliaSyntax releases.

This is currently awkward because we're basically versioning two APIs:

1. There's the JuliaSyntax package version for our `SyntaNode` AST and APIs like `parsestmt()`, etc. Next release will be #472, we have a couple more breaking changes to make.
2. There's the version number for `Expr`-related functionality used by Base - this is semantically just Julia version number (apart from bugfixes specific to this package)

These two versions serve really different purposes and may not even have compatible orderings.

For example, #456 made a change to operator kinds in the AST which is completely invisible to `Expr`, but is a breaking change to the JuliaSyntax API and should be followed on with #474 for consistency before the next JuliaSyntax release.  Conversely, the new functionality introduced by #456 is only a feature addition.

There's one big mitigating factor: JuliaSyntax's `parsestmt()` has a `version` which is independent of Julia's `VERSION`, so in principle any new version of JuliaSyntax can be used with an old version of Julia.

I hope that getting to a solid JuliaSyntax 1.0.0 release soon will get things less diverged and easier to manage.

There's one extra factor - for the purposes of maintaining a Julia LTS, we might want an LTS branch of JuliaSyntax itself to support the associated Julia LTS. This is partly a sign that our testing still needs work but being conservative with patches for the LTS is probably a good thing.

### What to do about the mess?

How about we do the following:
* Get JuliaSyntax to 1.0 asap, and try to cut releases off `main` more often. If we need a 2.0 soon, so be it (we will; JuliaSyntax's API will keep evolving, it's not close to done yet.
* Potentially, keep an release-lts-1.10 branch for Julia 1.10 LTS
* Use the latest JuliaSyntax releases when vendoring into Julia Base dev versions

This last one is a bit of an unknown. We don't want to hold Julia Base development up by having JuliaSyntax's release cycle stuck like it has been lately. So alternatively we could just tag JuliaSyntax with a Base-specific version tag at any time that someone bumps the vendored version in Base.  But then we have even more versions flying around and that seems bad too.

### Tooling

* There's a hacky script I use for bumping the vendored version in Base. This should at least go in this repo in the tools directory. Or maybe in Base itself.
* It'd be nice to have regression tests of JuliaSyntax against itself for both `SyntaxNode` and for `Expr`. @LilithHafner had some good ideas about this. At some point we also need to stop testing against the flisp parser as the source of truth. Especially as we add features, but also because fixing bugs in things like line numbers makes maintaining the code which compares with the flisp parser pretty tedious and error prone.
* We really need better tooling for regression testing against the corpus of Julia code in the package ecosystem. We should be doing this for `Expr` parsing (at least) each time we release a JuliaSyntax version. At the moment this is a problem because downloading and unpacking the entire package ecosystem is slow. And we still test against the flisp parser which is a problem because there are exceptions I manually look through.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/481/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/481/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/comments/2267264178","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/481#issuecomment-2267264178","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/481","id":2267264178,"node_id":"IC_kwDOGankvM6HI7Sy","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-04T02:48:35Z","updated_at":"2024-08-04T02:48:35Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"Yeah so we could just tag JuliaSyntax `main` branch with a Base-specific tag when people want to bump it. It'd be good if in this case we gave a tip.

This isn't the right approach, but some abandoned code from a cruder example had some suggested language.
```
function check_for_prompt_start(line::String)
    if startswith(line, JULIA_PROMPT)
        printstyled("Tip:"; color=info_color())
        println(" No need to type `$JULIA_PROMPT`; enter your commands directly.")
    elseif startswith(line, PKG_PROMPT)
        printstyled("Tip:"; color=info_color())
        println(" To switch to the `$PKG_PROMPT` prompt, press `]` rather than typing `$PKG_PROMPT`.")
    elseif startswith(line, SHELL_PROMPT)
        printstyled("Tip:"; color=info_color())
        println(" To switch to the `$SHELL_PROMPT` prompt, press `;` rather than typing `$SHELL_PROMPT`.")
    elseif startswith(line, HELP_PROMPT)
        printstyled("Tip:"; color=info_color())
        println(" To switch to the `$HELP_PROMPT` prompt, press `?` rather than typing `$HELP_PROMPT`.")
    end
end
```

{"action":"opened","number":483,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pulls/483","id":2007500188,"node_id":"PR_kwDOGankvM53qAWc","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/483","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/483.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/483.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/483","number":483,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Rename `haschildren()` to `is_leaf()`","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"Unfortunately, `haschildren(x)` was a terrible name because it's not testing the same thing as `numchildren(x) == 0`!

In our ASTs
* Leaves of the tree correspond to tokens in the source text
* Internal nodes are containers for a range of tokens or other internal nodes.

Occasionally we can have internal nodes which have no tokens and thus have `numchildren(node) == 0`. These are, however, still "internal nodes" and we have `haschildren(node) === true` for these which makes no In our ASTs
* Leaves of the tree correspond to tokens in the source text
* Internal nodes are containers for a range of tokens or other internal nodes.

Occasionally we can have internal nodes which have no tokens and thus have `numchildren(node) == 0`. These are, however, still "internal nodes" and we have `haschildren(node) === true` for these which makes no {"repository_id":430564540,"push_id":19659829840,"size":1,"distinct_size":1,"ref":"refs/heads/main","head":"d8796c6ac136c43aba2283da622a25c3c46708bf","before":"38fc0fa6f17fb1ac25350bfc0928b78cd5b616b9","commits":[{"sha":"d8796c6ac136c43aba2283da622a25c3c46708bf","author":{"email":"0d1bb7fa6b1aefd25edab787ad0bbdf687df277d@gmail.com","name":"Claire Foster"},"message":"Rename `haschildren()` to `is_leaf()` (#483)

Unfortunately, `haschildren(x)` was a terrible name because it's not
testing the same thing as `numchildren(x) == 0`!

In our ASTs
* Leaves of the tree correspond to tokens in the source text
* Internal nodes are containers for a range of tokens or other internal
  nodes.

Occasionally we can have internal nodes which have no tokens and thus
have `numchildren(node) == 0`. These are, however, still "internal
nodes" and we have `haschildren(node) === true` for these which makes no
sense!","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/commits/d8796c6ac136c43aba2283da622a25c3c46708bf"}]}

{"repository_id":430564540,"push_id":19660810214,"size":2,"distinct_size":2,"ref":"refs/heads/caf/tree-API-cleanup","head":"308cc7651f8a8fdb12382bc9b0b41bfb60608a34","before":"1734151fa935e559fa8ff9415e29ab5c604617a4","commits":[{"sha":"98843df91eb9a613e93581910415ceb7dbbe40b1","author":{"email":"0d1bb7fa6b1aefd25edab787ad0bbdf687df277d@gmail.com","name":"Claire Foster"},"message":"Clean up and document syntax tree child access API

Here I commit to a more consistent but simpler child access API for
syntax trees, as informed by the JuliaLowering work so far:

* `is_leaf(node)` is given a precise definition (previously
  `!haschildren()` - but that had issues - see #483)
* `children(node)` returns the child list, or `nothing` if there are no
  children. The `nothing` might be seen as inconvenient, but mapping
  across the children of a leaf node is probably an error and one should
  probably branch on `is_leaf` first.
* `numchildren(node)` is documented
* `node[i]`, `node[i:j]` are documented to index into the child list

We distinguish `GreenNode` and its implementation of `span` from
`SyntaxNode` and its implementation of `byte_range` and `sourcetext` -
these seem to just have very different APIs, at least as of now.

I've deleted the questionable overloads of multidimensional `getindex`
and the `child` function in favor of single dimensional getindex. I
don't know whether anyone ever ended up using these. But I didn't and
they didn't seem useful+consistent enough to keep the complexity.

I've kept setindex! for now, to set a child of a `SyntaxNode`. Though
I'm not sure this is a good idea to support by default.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/commits/98843df91eb9a613e93581910415ceb7dbbe40b1"},{"sha":"308cc7651f8a8fdb12382bc9b0b41bfb60608a34","author":{"email":"0d1bb7fa6b1aefd25edab787ad0bbdf687df277d@gmail.com","name":"Claire Foster"},"message":"Mark public symbols with `public` keyword","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/commits/308cc7651f8a8fdb12382bc9b0b41bfb60608a34"}]}

{"repository_id":430564540,"push_id":19663719598,"size":2,"distinct_size":2,"ref":"refs/heads/caf/tree-API-cleanup","head":"57c8c3c83ad0fb409683f35e54c2714b1444c842","before":"6cd1b85426ee8c5c10c72538e6b41b6f9282f982","commits":[{"sha":"784aa8c4829c7a4f7b259084dd4c44181f9ab4b5","author":{"email":"0d1bb7fa6b1aefd25edab787ad0bbdf687df277d@gmail.com","name":"Claire Foster"},"message":"Clean up and document syntax tree child access API

Here I commit to a more consistent but simpler child access API for
syntax trees, as informed by the JuliaLowering work so far:

* `is_leaf(node)` is given a precise definition (previously
  `!haschildren()` - but that had issues - see #483)
* `children(node)` returns the child list, or `nothing` if there are no
  children. The `nothing` might be seen as inconvenient, but mapping
  across the children of a leaf node is probably an error and one should
  probably branch on `is_leaf` first.
* `numchildren(node)` is documented
* `node[i]`, `node[i:j]` are documented to index into the child list

We distinguish `GreenNode` and its implementation of `span` from
`SyntaxNode` and its implementation of `byte_range` and `sourcetext` -
these seem to just have very different APIs, at least as of now.

I've deleted the questionable overloads of multidimensional `getindex`
and the `child` function in favor of single dimensional getindex. I
don't know whether anyone ever ended up using these. But I didn't and
they didn't seem useful+consistent enough to keep the complexity.

I've kept setindex! for now, to set a child of a `SyntaxNode`. Though
I'm not sure this is a good idea to support by default.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/commits/784aa8c4829c7a4f7b259084dd4c44181f9ab4b5"},{"sha":"57c8c3c83ad0fb409683f35e54c2714b1444c842","author":{"email":"0d1bb7fa6b1aefd25edab787ad0bbdf687df277d@gmail.com","name":"Claire Foster"},"message":"Mark public symbols with `public` keyword","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/commits/57c8c3c83ad0fb409683f35e54c2714b1444c842"}]}

{"action":"closed","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/457","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/457/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/457/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/457/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/457","id":2402729689,"node_id":"I_kwDOGankvM6PNr7Z","number":457,"title":"`char_range()` function for indexing into source strings","user":{"login":"davidanthoff","id":1036561,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjEwMzY1NjE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/1036561?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidanthoff","html_url":"https://github.com/davidanthoff","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidanthoff/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidanthoff/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidanthoff/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidanthoff/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidanthoff/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidanthoff/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidanthoff/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidanthoff/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/davidanthoff/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":12,"created_at":"2024-07-11T09:36:33Z","updated_at":"2024-08-09T01:51:22Z","closed_at":"2024-08-09T01:51:22Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"We have a crash from the VS Code extension that seems to originate from JuliaSyntax. Repo steps are:
1. `pkg> dev Sunny`
2. Run
```julia
using JuliaSyntax

src = read(joinpath(homedir(), ".julia/dev/Sunny/test/test_tensors.jl"), String)

tree = parseall(SyntaxNode, src, filename="foo.jl")

i = JuliaSyntax.last_byte(tree[2][end][end])

src[i]
```

That crashes with
```
ERROR: StringIndexError: invalid index [2142], valid nearby indices [2140]=>'′', [2143]=>'\r'
```

I assume that `last_byte` should always return a valid index, right? So this seems like a bug.

The rest of this issue is some speculation from my end, might all be wrong :)

I started looking a bit around in the JuliaSyntax code, and there seems to be a fair bit of code that assumes 1-byte characters, which strikes me as incorrect? Or maybe this is carefully only done when there is a guarantee that only 1-byte codepoints can appear? An example is https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/blob/1d950817b8ab62a3be2464f4d5b097aa68c17042/src/parse_stream.jl#L519 Doesn't that (incorrectly) assume that the character at index position `last_byte` is only one code unit? Generally if I search the repo for `- 1` or `+ 1` I see a fair bit of code where I would have assumed that a `prevind` or `nextind` would be needed?","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/457/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/457/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"}}

API","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-08-07T07:31:47Z","updated_at":"2024-08-09T01:54:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pulls/484","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/484","diff_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/484.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/484.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"Here I commit to a more consistent but simpler child access API for syntax trees, as informed by the JuliaLowering work so far:

* `is_leaf(node)` is given a precise definition (previously `!haschildren()` - but that had issues - see #483)
* `children(node)` returns the child list, or `nothing` if there are no children. The `nothing` might be seen as inconvenient, but mapping across the children of a leaf node is probably an error and one should probably branch on `is_leaf` first.
* `numchildren(node)` is documented
* `node[i]`, `node[i:j]` are documented to index into the child list

We distinguish `GreenNode` and its implementation of `span` from `SyntaxNode` and its implementation of `byte_range` and `sourcetext` - these seem to just have very different APIs, at least as of now.

I've deleted the questionable overloads of multidimensional `getindex` and the `child` function in favor of single dimensional getindex. I don't know whether anyone ever ended up using these. But I didn't and they didn't seem useful+consistent enough to keep the complexity.

I've kept setindex! for now, to set a child of a `SyntaxNode`. Though I'm not sure this is a good idea to support by default and I didn't mark it public yet.

Also I've added more docs!

Also mark the public parts of the API as `public` in Julia 1.11+","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/484/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/484/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/comments/2277003798","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/484#issuecomment-2277003798","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/484","id":2277003798,"node_id":"IC_kwDOGankvM6HuFIW","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-09T01:54:02Z","updated_at":"2024-08-09T01:54:02Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> I presume this will become 0.5.0?

We need to get to 1.0 so we can use semver properly.  So the next release will be 1.0.0 :-)  I've been working really hard on a lot of 1.0.0 changes in the last few weeks, trying to get the documentation and APIs in better shape.  See also #472, but I keep finding more to do - it turns out there's a lot of changes I want to squeeze in which are informed by the JuliaLowering work.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/comments/2277003798/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

I commit to a more consistent but simpler child access API for syntax trees, as informed by the JuliaLowering work so far:

* `is_leaf(node)` is given a precise definition (previously `!haschildren()` - but that had issues - see #483)
* `children(node)` returns the child list, or `nothing` if there are no children. The `nothing` might be seen as inconvenient, but mapping across the children of a leaf node is probably an error and one should probably branch on `is_leaf` first.
* `numchildren(node)` is documented
* `node[i]`, `node[i:j]` are documented to index into the child list

We distinguish `GreenNode` and its implementation of `span` from `SyntaxNode` and its implementation of `byte_range` and `sourcetext` - these seem to just have very different APIs, at least as of now.

I've deleted the questionable overloads of multidimensional `getindex` and the `child` function in favor of single dimensional getindex. I don't know whether anyone ever ended up using these. But I didn't and they didn't seem useful+consistent enough to keep the complexity.

I've kept setindex! for now, to set a child of a `SyntaxNode`. Though I'm not sure this is a good idea to support by default and I didn't mark it public yet.

Also I've added more docs!

Also mark the public parts of the API as `public` in Julia 1.11+","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/484/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/484/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/comments/2277006222","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/pull/484#issuecomment-2277006222","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/issues/484","id":2277006222,"node_id":"IC_kwDOGankvM6HuFuO","user":{"login":"c42f","id":601473,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjYwMTQ3Mw==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/601473?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f","html_url":"https://github.com/c42f","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/c42f/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-08-09T01:56:03Z","updated_at":"2024-08-09T01:56:03Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"> I think TypedSyntax's tests do exploit multidimensional child, but that's an easy change.

Here I commit to a more consistent but simpler child access API for
syntax trees, as informed by the JuliaLowering work so far:

* `is_leaf(node)` is given a precise definition (previously
  `!haschildren()` - but that had issues - see #483)
* `children(node)` returns the child list, or `nothing` if there are no
  children. The `nothing` might be seen as inconvenient, but mapping
  across the children of a leaf node is probably an error and one should
  probably branch on `is_leaf` first.
* `numchildren(node)` is documented
* `node[i]`, `node[i:j]` are documented to index into the child list

We distinguish `GreenNode` and its implementation of `span` from
`SyntaxNode` and its implementation of `byte_range` and `sourcetext` -
these seem to just have very different APIs, at least as of now.

I've deleted the questionable overloads of multidimensional `getindex`
and the `child` function in favor of single dimensional getindex. I
don't know whether anyone ever ended up using these. But I didn't and
they didn't seem useful+consistent enough to keep the complexity.

I've kept setindex! for now, to set a child of a `SyntaxNode`. Though
I'm not sure this is a good idea to support by default.","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/commits/497cd63505f0f7a7cd34e3ed0df99554a32cac46"},{"sha":"b691d4ced297c69acbd19908340e57eafa07ac58","author":{"email":"0d1bb7fa6b1aefd25edab787ad0bbdf687df277d@gmail.com","name":"Claire Foster"},"message":"Mark public symbols with `public` keyword","distinct":true,"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/JuliaSyntax.jl/commits/b691d4ced297c69acbd19908340e57eafa07ac58"},{"sha":"5aa4740b8af900c882dc157d000b75da4928e28f","author":{"email":"0d1bb7fa6b1aefd25edab787ad0bbdf687df277d@gmail.com","name":"Claire Foster"},"message":"fixup!
